---
title: "Exercise: GC content across a batch"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Given a batch of integer-encoded DNA sequences of shape `[N, L]` (A=0, C=1, G=2, T=3), return a 1-D float tensor of length `N` where entry `i` is the **GC fraction** [the proportion of bases in sequence `i` that are G or C, between 0 and 1] of that sequence.

By the time `cargo test` passes you will have used [`equal_elem`](https://docs.rs/burn/latest/burn/tensor/struct.Tensor.html#method.equal_elem) (gloss: element-wise comparison against a scalar — like `x == 2` in NumPy), a boolean → float cast, and [`mean_dim`](https://docs.rs/burn/latest/burn/tensor/struct.Tensor.html#method.mean_dim).

## Background

You wrote a per-sequence GC counter on day 1 — a `for` loop over the bytes of one string. Today we do it for **a batch of `N` sequences at once**, with no `for` loops at all. The pattern is the daily idiom of tensor programming:

1. Compute a per-element boolean tensor: `is_gc[i][j] = seqs[i][j] == G || seqs[i][j] == C`.
2. Cast booleans to floats: `0.0` or `1.0`.
3. Reduce along the length axis with `mean`.

The PyTorch one-liner:

```python
gc = ((seqs == 1) | (seqs == 2)).float().mean(dim=1)     # [N]
```

The Burn version of the same three steps:

```rust
let is_c = seqs.clone().equal_elem(1);                   // [N, L] bool
let is_g = seqs.equal_elem(2);                           // [N, L] bool
let is_gc = (is_c.int() + is_g.int()).float();           // [N, L] float (0.0 or 1.0)
let gc: Tensor<B, 1> = is_gc.mean_dim(1).squeeze_dim(1);     // [N]
```

(You can also use bitwise OR on the bool tensors directly — but mapping to int and adding is simpler to read and avoids a few API gotchas.)

## The exercise

Open `day11/ex-gc-content/`. Implement `gc_content<B: Backend>(seqs: Tensor<B, 2, Int>) -> Tensor<B, 1>` so the tests pass. The starter code reuses `seq_to_ints` and `build_batch` from exercise 2.

You may **not** change the function signature or the tests.

## How to run

```bash
cd day11/ex-gc-content
cargo test
cargo run
```

When the body is right you will see:

```
test result: ok. 5 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — equal_elem on an Int tensor"}
[`equal_elem(value)`](https://docs.rs/burn/latest/burn/tensor/struct.Tensor.html#method.equal_elem) returns a *boolean* tensor of the same shape. To turn it into a 0/1 numeric tensor that you can sum or mean, chain `.int()` or `.float()`:

```rust
let is_c: Tensor<B, 2, Int>  = seqs.clone().equal_elem(1).int();
let is_g: Tensor<B, 2, Int>  = seqs.equal_elem(2).int();
let is_gc: Tensor<B, 2>      = (is_c + is_g).float();         // 0.0 or 1.0
```

Note: at most one of "is C" and "is G" is true for any base, so adding them never overflows past 1.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — mean over the length axis"}
`mean_dim(1)` averages along the length axis. For `[N, L]` input it returns `[N, 1]`:

```rust
let gc_2d: Tensor<B, 2> = is_gc.mean_dim(1);            // [N, 1]
let gc:    Tensor<B, 1> = gc_2d.squeeze_dim(1);             // [N]
```

The PyTorch equivalent is `is_gc.mean(dim=1)` — note PyTorch drops the size-1 axis by default, Burn keeps it.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — sanity-check the numbers"}
For sequence `"GCGCGC"` (length 6): all bases are G or C, so `gc = 1.0`. For `"AAAA"`: zero. For `"ACGT"`: one G and one C out of four, so `gc = 0.5`. The unit tests use these.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```rust
fn gc_content<B: Backend>(seqs: Tensor<B, 2, Int>) -> Tensor<B, 1> {
    let is_c = seqs.clone().equal_elem(1).int();
    let is_g = seqs.equal_elem(2).int();
    let is_gc = (is_c + is_g).float();
    is_gc.mean_dim(1).squeeze_dim(1)
}
```

The full reference is in `day11/ex-gc-content-solution/src/main.rs`.
:::

## Where this leads

- **k-mer counts** are the same pattern with a different reduction: build a mask per k-mer, sum along length.
- **Per-position base frequencies** across a batch are `oh.mean_dim(0)` on the one-hot from exercise 2 — `[4, L]` of position-specific base probabilities (a PWM!).
- **Exercise 4 (distance matrix)** moves on to multi-axis broadcasting, where the pattern is the same but spans more dimensions.
